text: Hello, I'm Joe Green, and I will be discussing the cosmetic cholecystectomy, how we can utilize robotics to achieve an aesthetically desirable outcome. These are my disclosures. The patient is a twenty-six year old female with a body mass index of twenty-five, who presents with biliridus kinesia for an elective cholecystectomy.

In the preoperative holding area, I ask the patient to identify where her bikini line lies, for incisional planning and mark it accordingly in the operating room the patient is positioned supine with the arms out the bed is flexed slightly cannulas one two and four will be placed along the bikini line with a working port arm three on the cephalid aspect of the umbilicus

The surgeon uses a peri-umbilical optical trocar technique to access the abdomen, followed by a laparoscopically guided transversus abdominis plane block. Cannulas are placed in pre-planned positions along the bikini line, and the patient is positioned in twelve degrees of reverse Trendelenburg.

The surgeon uses a bipolar forceps in arm one, a hook cautery in arm three, and a prograsp in arm four, with the camera positioned in arm two. The gallbladder is retracted cephalad, revealing adhesions to the omentum. Monopolar cautery is employed to carefully dissect these adhesions while maintaining awareness of the duodenum's position below to prevent injury.

The surgeon retracts the gallbladder cephalad while allowing it to descend towards the bowel table, initiating a domed-down technique. The gallbladder is grasped in a caudal direction, and the hook cautery is used to incise the peritoneum, developing a plane between the gallbladder and the liver.

The surgeon retracts the gallbladder cephalad and proceeds with the dissection of Calot's triangle using a hook cautery in a lateral to medial orientation. The gallbladder is carefully separated from the liver bed in a bloodless plane, preserving the integrity of both the liver and gallbladder. Indocyanine green fluorescence imaging is utilized to enhance visualization of the gallbladder during the procedure.

The surgeon continues dissection along the avascular plane at the neck of the gallbladder, carefully removing adhesions while avoiding the duodenum. A lateral to medial dissection is performed to achieve the critical view of safety. The cystic artery and duct are identified using indocyanine green fluorescence imaging, and a window is created between them. The gallbladder is dissected away from the liver in the medial plane, achieving one-third separation to ensure a clear view. A doublet technique confirms the identification of the cystic structures entering the gallbladder.

The surgeon clips the cystic artery twice and the cystic duct three times, with an additional clip placed on the gallbladder side. The cystic duct is then transected, followed by the transection of the cystic artery using an electric cautery device.

The surgeon uses hook electrocautery to remove the gallbladder from the liver, carefully maintaining the avascular plane between the two structures. The dissection is bloodless, preserving the integrity of both the gallbladder and the liver.

The surgeon inserts an endocatch bag percutaneously and deposits the completely removed gallbladder into it, ensuring no spillage of biliary contents. The extraction site is closed using a transvascular suture passer in a figure of eight fashion, resulting in a neat cosmetic outcome.
